metadata.description: Aggregate demographic data on open-source contributors from published surveys and research
sources: Tidelift State of the Open Source Maintainer 2024
Vasilescu et al. 2015 (Gender and Tenure in GitHub)
Terrell et al. 2017 (Gender Differences in PRs)
metadata.caveats: All demographic data has significant limitations. Survey data is self-selected. Name-based gender inference has ~70-85% accuracy and is binary/Western-biased. Geographic data from profiles is self-reported (~60-70% fill rate).

gender_distribution.notes: Self-reported. Survey has known selection bias toward English-speaking, professional developers.
gender_distribution.notes_on_trend: Essentially flat since 2020. The 2018-2019 higher numbers may reflect survey methodology changes, not real gains. [VERIFY: exact percentages from each year's survey]

geographic_distribution.notes: [VERIFY: all percentages from 2024 Octoverse]. Key insight: US 5x overrepresented, India 1.3x underrepresented, Africa massively underrepresented (2x in Nigeria, much worse elsewhere).

linux_kernel.notes: Most kernel developers are employed by Intel, Red Hat, Google, Samsung, etc.

general_oss_maintainers.notes: Tidelift 2024: 74% of maintainers are unpaid. But 90% of Linux kernel contributors are paid. The gap tells the whole story.

corporate_vs_volunteer.key_finding: Critical infrastructure is funded. Everything else is volunteer labor.

pr_acceptance_research.source: Terrell et al. 2017, PeerJ Computer Science
pr_acceptance_research.finding: Women's PRs accepted at higher rate (78.6%) than men's (74.6%) when gender is NOT identifiable from profile. When gender IS identifiable, women's acceptance rate drops to 62.5%.
pr_acceptance_research.notes: Landmark study on gender bias in code review. Widely cited but also criticized for methodological choices in gender inference.

mobile_os_market_share.insight: Android (open source) dominates where incomes are lower. iOS dominates in wealthy countries. Open source literally provides mobile computing to billions who couldn't afford proprietary alternatives.
